why: why is it called a tree
how: how is html structured in memory

1_beginner_explanation.summary: The browser organizes the DOM into an upside-down family tree. At the very top is the `document` root. Below that is the `<html>` element, which splits into two branches: `<head>` and `<body>`. The `<body>` might branch into a `<div>` and a `<ul>`, and the `<ul>` branches into `<li>` items. Every single piece of this tree is called a **Node**.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: Think of a real tree turned upside down. The trunk is the `document`. The major thick branches are `html` and `body`. The smaller branches are `divs` and `sections`. The leaves at the very end of the branches are the actual text words you see on the screen.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: The browser uses a Tree data structure because it allows for incredibly fast traversal and cascading rules. When you apply a CSS style to the `<body>` branch, the engine quickly trickles that style down to all descendant nodes connected to that branch.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Every Node contains pointers (memory addresses) to its Parent Node, its First Child Node, its Last Child Node, and its Sibling Nodes. This web of pointers is what forms the 'Tree'.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Understanding how nested HTML maps to JavaScript objects.
3_real_world_example.code:
/* HTML Structure:

Hello

*/ const p = document.querySelector('p'); // Because it's a tree, we can walk UP the branches console.log(p.parentElement.id); // 'container' console.log(p.parentElement.parentElement.tagName); // 'BODY'

4_common_confusion.trap: Thinking that only HTML tags are Nodes. The DOM tree is incredibly strict. Even the blank spaces (line breaks) you hit on your keyboard between HTML tags are converted into invisible 'Text Nodes'.
4_common_confusion.fix:
A Node can be an Element (a tag like `

`), a Text Node (the words 'Hello' or just empty space), or a Comment Node (``). Therefore, `Node !== Element`.

6_edge_case.quirk: The `document` node is the root of the tree, but it is NOT an HTML Element. It sits *above* the `<html>` element.
